content: TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) is a connection-oriented reliable transport protocol. It establishes connections via a three-way handshake: the client sends SYN, the server replies SYN+ACK, and the client sends ACK. After connection establishment, sequence and acknowledgment numbers ensure ordered delivery. TCP uses a sliding window for flow control and a congestion window for congestion control.

TCP congestion control algorithms include Slow Start, Congestion Avoidance, Fast Retransmit, and Fast Recovery. During slow start, the congestion window (cwnd) doubles for each ACK received; after reaching the threshold, congestion avoidance takes over with linear cwnd growth. Three duplicate ACKs trigger fast retransmit, halving the threshold to the current cwnd.

TCP reliable transmission relies on Retransmission Timeout (RTO) and Selective Acknowledgment (SACK). RTO is dynamically calculated from RTT samples using the Karn algorithm to avoid ambiguity. SACK lets the receiver inform the sender which segments have been received, reducing unnecessary retransmissions. Keep-Alive probes periodically check connection liveness.

UDP (User Datagram Protocol) is a connectionless transport protocol without reliability guarantees, flow control, or congestion control. Its header is only 8 bytes (source port, destination port, length, checksum), resulting in minimal overhead. It is ideal for real-time audio/video, DNS queries, gaming, and other latency-sensitive scenarios. UDP supports broadcast and multicast.

UDP does not guarantee delivery order or delivery at all. Applications must handle packet loss and reordering themselves. Common solutions include application-layer ARQ (e.g., QUIC), Forward Error Correction (FEC), and Jitter Buffers. UDP maximum datagram length is limited by the IP layer MTU; datagrams exceeding the MTU are fragmented at the IP level.

HTTP/2 is the second major version of HTTP, introducing multiplexing, header compression (HPACK), and server push over HTTP/1.1. Multiplexing allows parallel transmission of multiple requests and responses on a single TCP connection, eliminating Head-of-Line Blocking. Frames are the smallest communication unit in HTTP/2, and Streams are the logical containers for frames.

HTTP/3 is based on QUIC (running over UDP), completely solving TCP-level Head-of-Line Blocking. QUIC has built-in TLS 1.3 encryption enabling 0-RTT or 1-RTT handshakes. Connection migration lets clients switch networks (e.g., Wi-Fi to cellular) without re-establishing connections. QUIC manages reliability per-stream, so packet loss on one stream does not affect others.

B+ Tree is the most common index structure in relational databases. All data is stored in leaf nodes, which form an ordered linked list via pointers for efficient range scans. Internal nodes store only keys and child pointers, allowing more keys per node and reducing tree height. InnoDB uses a default page size of 16KB; a three-level B+ Tree can store approximately 20 million rows.

Hash indexes map keys to buckets using a hash function with O(1) lookup complexity. However, hash indexes do not support range queries or sorting. InnoDB's Adaptive Hash Index (AHI) automatically builds hash indexes on top of B+ Tree indexes based on access patterns, accelerating equality lookups. AHI is fully automatic and requires no DBA intervention.

MySQL MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control) is implemented via undo logs. Each row contains hidden DB_TRX_ID (last modifying transaction ID) and DB_ROLL_PTR (pointer to undo log). A Read View is created when a transaction starts, recording the list of active transactions. During queries, data version visibility is determined by the Read View: if the row's TRX_ID is less than the smallest active transaction ID, it is visible.

Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) automatically extract spatial features through convolutional layers. The convolution operation slides learnable filters (kernels) over the input feature map, computing weighted sums of local regions. Common convolution types include standard convolution, Depthwise Separable convolution, and Dilated (Atrous) convolution. Pooling layers reduce parameters via downsampling, commonly using max pooling or average pooling.

The Transformer model is based on Self-Attention, eliminating RNN sequential dependencies. The core formula is Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QK^T/sqrt(d_k))V. Multi-Head Attention projects input into multiple subspaces, computing attention separately then concatenating, capturing feature relationships across different dimensions. Positional Encoding injects sequence position information via sinusoidal functions.

Backpropagation computes gradients of the loss function with respect to each parameter using the chain rule. The Computational Graph records all forward pass operations; during backward traversal, gradients are computed layer by layer. Vanishing gradients are common in deep networks; solutions include residual connections (ResNet), Layer Normalization, and Gradient Clipping. Adam optimizer combines momentum and adaptive learning rate as the most widely used optimizer.

The Linux Virtual File System (VFS) provides a unified abstraction interface for different file systems. VFS defines four core objects: superblock, inode, dentry, and file object. All file systems must implement VFS-defined operation interfaces (read, write, open, etc.); the kernel invokes each file system's implementation through function pointers.

The ext4 file system replaces traditional indirect block mapping with Extent Trees, significantly improving large file performance. An Extent describes a contiguous range of physical blocks, reducing metadata overhead. ext4 supports Delayed Allocation, which defers physical block allocation until data flush, optimizing disk layout and reducing fragmentation. Journaling supports three modes: ordered, writeback, and journal.

Page Cache is the core file I/O optimization in the Linux kernel. On file read, data is cached in the page cache; subsequent reads return directly from memory. On write, data is written to the page cache and returns immediately (async write); dirty pages are periodically flushed by pdflush/writeback threads. The dirty page ratio threshold is configurable via /proc/sys/vm/dirty_ratio.

Raft is a distributed consensus algorithm that is easier to understand and implement than Paxos. Raft decomposes the consensus problem into three sub-problems: Leader Election, Log Replication, and Safety. Each node in the cluster is in one of three states: Leader, Follower, or Candidate. The Leader receives client requests and replicates log entries to Followers.

Raft leader election: when a Follower times out without receiving a Leader heartbeat, it becomes a Candidate and initiates voting (RequestVote RPC). Each Term has at most one Leader. A Candidate receiving majority votes becomes Leader. Log replication: the Leader replicates log entries to Followers via AppendEntries RPC; entries are committed once confirmed by a majority. Log continuity is guaranteed by prevLogIndex and prevLogTerm.

Paxos is divided into Basic Paxos and Multi-Paxos. Basic Paxos achieves consensus on a single value in two phases: in the Prepare phase, the Proposer sends a proposal number and the Acceptor promises not to accept smaller numbers; in the Accept phase, the Proposer sends a value and consensus is reached when accepted by a majority. Multi-Paxos optimizes consecutive value consensus by electing a stable Leader to skip the Prepare phase.

Container technology is based on Linux kernel Namespaces and Cgroups for resource isolation. Namespaces provide six isolation dimensions: PID (process), NET (network), MNT (filesystem mount), UTS (hostname), IPC (inter-process communication), and USER (user ID). Each container has independent process trees, network stacks, and filesystem views without interference.

Cgroups (Control Groups) limit and monitor container resource usage. The CPU subsystem uses CFS bandwidth control to limit CPU time slices; the Memory subsystem sets memory limits (memory.limit_in_bytes) with OOM Killer triggered on exceeding limits; the Blkio subsystem limits disk I/O bandwidth. Cgroup v2 unifies the hierarchy and introduces PSI (Pressure Stall Information) for resource pressure monitoring.

Docker images use Union File Systems (UnionFS/OverlayFS) for layered storage. Each image layer is read-only; a writable Container Layer is added on top at runtime. Multiple containers can share the same underlying image layers, saving storage. docker build constructs images layer by layer from Dockerfile instructions, with Layer Cache accelerating repeated builds.

Redis uses a single-threaded event-driven model to process client requests. The event loop (aeEventLoop) uses epoll/kqueue to listen for read/write events on file descriptors. Despite being single-threaded, Redis achieves high throughput (100k+ QPS per instance) through in-memory operations, efficient data structures, and I/O multiplexing. Redis 6.0 introduced I/O multithreading for network reads/writes while keeping command execution single-threaded.

Redis persistence provides RDB and AOF mechanisms. RDB generates memory snapshots via fork, leveraging Copy-on-Write to reduce memory overhead. AOF logs every write command and replays them on restart for recovery. AOF rewrite compresses the log file size in a background thread. Redis 4.0 supports hybrid RDB+AOF persistence: during AOF rewrite, an RDB snapshot is written first, then incremental commands are appended.

Redis Cluster uses 16384 hash slots for data sharding. Each node is responsible for a subset of slots; keys are mapped to slots via the CRC16 algorithm. When a client sends a command to the wrong node, the node returns a MOVED or ASK redirect. The cluster exchanges node state via the Gossip protocol, supporting automatic failure detection and master-slave failover.
